text: The surgeon begins the ventral hernia repair by widely draping the surgical area. A blunt tip trocar is inserted in the right subcostal area under direct laparoscopic visualization. Tenaculums are used on the skin for counter traction as the trocar is wiggled into the peritoneum. Carbon dioxide is insufflated to create space, allowing the trocar to be fully inserted. This port will serve as one of the robotic arms, starting with a twelve millimeter standard laparoscopic port.

The surgeon positions the patient supine and places the laparoscopic camera port laterally to avoid arm clashing, using a long trocar for the robotic arm attachment. The camera arm is attached to the camera trocar, and the robotic port is positioned in the lower right abdomen with a long trocar for secure attachment. The remote center is positioned into the muscle layer, and the robot is docked, with the right hand dissection port connected to an eight and a half millimeter trocar.

The surgeon uses a 30-degree laparoscope to visualize the abdominal wall and begins dissection with a vessel sealer in the first robotic arm. The fenestrated bipolar instrument in the second arm is used for atraumatic grasping of the peritoneum and controlling any oozing during dissection. The procedure starts with freeing the abdominal wall from adhesions, particularly around the median umbilical ligaments, to prepare for mesh placement.

The surgeon uses an advanced bipolar device to coagulate and cut tissue, employing its blunt tip for dissection. The fascia is being freed to expose the fascial defect, with the intent to close it primarily and place mesh. The surgeon clears the fascial edges of fatty tissue and reduces the omentum incarcerated in the hernia.

The surgeon uses blunt and sharp dissection to release the omentum incarcerated in the hernia near the umbilical area. The base of the falciform ligament is visible. A 9 cm round Paritex polyester mesh with a collagen adhesion barrier is prepared for placement after primarily closing the defect. The mesh will be secured using sutures, tacks, or trans-fascial sutures.

The surgeon strips back the peritoneum to expose the fascia, preparing to place the mesh directly onto the fascia. A large needle is used to take a generous bite of the fascia, starting inferior to the apex of the defect. The V-lock suture is utilized, with its tail end eye allowing for the creation of a knotless closure by doubling back the suture on itself. The surgeon places multiple suture throws before tightening, allowing carbon dioxide to escape and the abdominal wall to relax, facilitating the closure of the fascial defect.

The surgeon uses a running suture to close the fascial defect, carefully sewing around the circumference of the defect with multiple passes through the fascia. The needle holder in the right hand has a scissor at the back of the jaw for cutting the suture at the end of the line. The surgeon pays attention to visual cues such as suture tension and instrument bending, ensuring a tight closure without gaps. Carbon dioxide is released to relax the abdominal wall, and the suture line is backed up on itself to secure the closure.

The surgeon backs up the suture line with two or three passes to create enough friction for the suture to lock. A large needle holder is used to position the Paritex polyester mesh with a collagen adhesion barrier over the fascial defect. The mesh is rolled with the adhesion barrier inside to prevent peeling and is centered over the primary suture line. Trans-fascial sutures are used to secure the mesh against the abdominal wall.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to sew around a complete 360-degree circle, maneuvering needle holders in various directions to achieve a secure closure. The robotic technique enhances dexterity, allowing the needle to be passed between hands for optimal angles during suturing.

The surgeon completes the suture line by overlapping the new suture with the previous one, ensuring tension is relieved without tying a knot. A 2-0 suture with a smaller needle is used to secure the mesh underlay over the primary closure, providing full coverage and preventing gaps.
